target.name: Mainspring Energy
target.founder: Shannon Miller, Adam Simpson, Matt Svrcek

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series F → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.3x) to $650M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $3.5B - $6.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Helion
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series F), post-money $5.4B

data.narrative:
Mainspring Energy is a Series F climate and energy technology company that has raised $650M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on two methodologies applied against a comparable set of 9 private and 1 public frontier-tech company in the climate and energy sector, anchored by three same-stage or adjacent-stage peers: Helion (Series F, $5.4B valuation), TAE Technologies (Series G, $6.0B), and Form Energy (Series G, $3.5B).

The stage-progression method, which applies a typical 1.3x step-up multiplier to the $650M raised proxy, implies a valuation of $845M. The peer-median approach, based on the median valuation of the three comparable companies, implies $5.4B, with a range of $3.5B to $6.0B. These two methods reflect material divergence: the stage-progression method anchors to capital invested, while the peer-median method reflects market pricing for similar-stage competitors in the sector.

Triangulating these results yields a low estimate of $718M, a median of $3.1B, and a high estimate of $6.2B. The wide range reflects structural uncertainty in frontier-energy valuations, where peer multiples on capital raised vary significantly based on technology maturity, pathway to commercialization, and market timing. Key uncertainties include the strength of Mainspring's commercialization roadmap relative to the peer set and whether recent capital market conditions have reset valuation multiples across the sector.
